name: Dome of the Rock
description: The Dome of the Rock is a significant Islamic shrine located on the Temple Mount in the Old City of Jerusalem. Completed in 691–692 CE, it is one of the oldest examples of Islamic architecture. The Dome of the Rock is built over the Foundation Stone, which holds immense religious significance in both Judaism and Islam. In Islamic tradition, it is believed to be the spot from which the Prophet Muhammad ascended to heaven during his Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj). The structure itself is not a mosque, but a shrine housing the sacred rock. Its golden dome and intricate mosaics make it a visually stunning landmark and a powerful symbol of Islam in Jerusalem. The Foundation Stone is also believed to be the place where Abraham prepared to sacrifice his son Isaac (or Ishmael, according to Islamic belief). The site represents a convergence of Abrahamic faiths and their shared history, making it a focal point of religious and political significance. The Dome of the Rock's architectural design and artistic ornamentation reflect the early development of Islamic art and its influence on subsequent architectural styles. The surrounding Temple Mount, known to Muslims as Haram al-Sharif (the Noble Sanctuary), is considered the third holiest site in Islam.
subtitle: Ascension Point and Sacred Foundation Stone

keyMyths.title: The Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj)
keyMyths.description: The Prophet Muhammad journeyed from Mecca to Jerusalem in a single night, then ascended to heaven from the Foundation Stone. During this journey, he met with prophets and received divine commandments.

keyMyths.title: Abraham's Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Muslims believe that Abraham prepared to sacrifice his son Ishmael (rather than Isaac) on the Foundation Stone as an act of obedience to God. This event is commemorated during Eid al-Adha.

sources.author: Al-Tabari
sources.work: History of the Prophets and Kings
sources.description: A comprehensive historical account including early Islamic traditions.
sources.text: A comprehensive historical account including early Islamic traditions.

sources.author: Ibn Kathir
sources.description: A renowned commentary on the Quran.
sources.text: A renowned commentary on the Quran.

worshipPractices.name: Prayer and Reflection
worshipPractices.description: Muslims visit the Dome of the Rock to offer prayers and reflect on the significance of the site in Islamic history and tradition. The Foundation Stone within the Dome is believed to be the place from which the Prophet Muhammad ascended to heaven during his Night Journey (Isra and Miraj), making it a sacred space for spiritual contemplation.

worshipPractices.name: Recitation of Quran
worshipPractices.description: The Dome of the Rock's interior often hosts recitations of the Quran, adding to the spiritual atmosphere and connecting visitors with the divine word. The beautiful calligraphy adorning the walls serves as a visual reminder of the Quranic verses, further enhancing the meditative experience.

festivals.name: Laylat al-Miraj (Night of Ascension)
festivals.timing: 27th day of the month of Rajab
festivals.description: This festival commemorates the Prophet Muhammad's Night Journey (Isra and Miraj), including his ascent to heaven from the Foundation Stone beneath the Dome of the Rock. While not directly celebrated at the Dome itself, its significance is highlighted during this time, with lectures, sermons, and discussions focusing on the event.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Dome of the Rock remains a powerful symbol of Islamic identity and a major pilgrimage site for Muslims worldwide. Its iconic golden dome and intricate architecture continue to inspire artists, architects, and writers, representing the rich cultural heritage of Islam and its connection to Jerusalem. It often features in media portrayals and discussions relating to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict due to its contested location and religious importance.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are considered sacred locations of immense religious importance, believed to be a bridge between the earthly and divine realms. They serve as focal points for pilgrimage and spiritual connection.

While Bifrost is a bridge, not a physical structure on a specific location, both serve as a connection between the mortal realm and the divine or spiritual plane. Both are considered points of access to higher powers.
Both are considered the center of the world in their respective mythologies. They represent a cosmic axis and a place of immense spiritual power and connection to the divine.

Both represent a primordial mound or stone from which creation began. The Dome of the Rock's Foundation Stone and the Benben Stone are both associated with the origins of the world and divine presence.

associations.name: Al-Buraq
associations.significance: It is believed that the Prophet Muhammad ascended to heaven from the Foundation Stone during the Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj) on Al-Buraq.

associations.name: Ascension
associations.significance: The Dome marks the site of the Prophet Muhammad's ascension, linking the earthly and divine realms.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven heavens are mentioned in the Quran, and the ascension through them connects with the Dome's symbolic importance.

associations.name: Light
associations.significance: The Dome is associated with divine light (Nur) due to its connection with the Prophet's spiritual journey and the presence of God.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: The golden dome symbolizes the radiance of faith and divine presence.

associations.name: Revelation
associations.significance: The Dome stands as a testament to the divine revelation received by the Prophet Muhammad.

associations.name: Paradise
associations.significance: The Dome of the Rock is viewed by some as an earthly representation or gateway to Paradise, due to the Prophet's journey.

companions.name: Archangel Gabriel (Jibril)
companions.description: Archangel Gabriel guided Prophet Muhammad during his Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj), leading him to Jerusalem and the Dome of the Rock site.

companions.name: Prophet Muhammad
companions.description: The Dome of the Rock commemorates Prophet Muhammad's Night Journey and ascension to heaven from the Foundation Stone.

companions.name: Al-Buraq
companions.description: Al-Buraq, a mythical creature, carried Prophet Muhammad from Mecca to Jerusalem during the Night Journey, enabling his arrival at the site of the Dome.

companions.name: Angels
companions.description: Angels are believed to protect and guard holy sites, including the Temple Mount and the Dome of the Rock.

companions.name: Umayyad Caliph Abd al-Malik
companions.description: Abd al-Malik commissioned the construction of the Dome of the Rock, ensuring its establishment and preservation as an important Islamic monument.

extendedContent.title: Geography and Supernatural Features of the Rock
extendedContent.content: The Foundation Stone, at the heart of the Dome of the Rock, is not merely a geological feature; it holds profound spiritual weight in Islamic cosmology. Islamic tradition identifies it as the highest point on Earth, connecting the terrestrial realm with the divine. Some accounts describe it as hovering slightly above the ground, a testament to its sacred nature and its role as the point of ascension for the Prophet Muhammad. It is believed that from this spot, the waters beneath the earth are held back, preventing a catastrophic flood. The physical rock itself is seen as a barrier, a divine intervention preventing chaos from engulfing the world. Furthermore, some interpretations suggest the rock contains a cave-like structure beneath it, referred to as the 'Well of Souls,' believed to be a place where the spirits of the deceased gather to pray. This cave connects the physical world with the spiritual realm, solidifying the Foundation Stone's role as a cosmic nexus. The geographical location of the Dome of the Rock, atop the Temple Mount in Jerusalem, further enhances its importance, placing it at the center of a region revered by multiple Abrahamic faiths.

extendedContent.title: The Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj) and the Dome of the Rock
extendedContent.content: The Dome of the Rock is inextricably linked to the Islamic narrative of the Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj) of Prophet Muhammad. According to Islamic tradition, during this miraculous journey, the angel Gabriel transported Muhammad from Mecca to Jerusalem in a single night. The Dome of the Rock marks the location where Muhammad is believed to have ascended to heaven. This event is recounted in the Quran (Surah Al-Isra, 17:1) and elaborated upon in Hadith literature. The Foundation Stone is said to bear the imprint of Muhammad's foot as he ascended. The experience of the Mi'raj involved Muhammad's journey through the seven heavens, where he met prophets such as Abraham, Moses, and Jesus, and ultimately encountered God. This journey is considered one of the most significant events in Islamic history and solidifies the Dome of the Rock's status as a sacred site, a physical manifestation of the Prophet's connection with the divine. The structure serves as a constant reminder of this pivotal event and its profound spiritual implications for Muslims worldwide.

extendedContent.title: Sacred Significance and the Seal of Prophets
extendedContent.content: The Dome of the Rock's sanctity stems from its association with several key Islamic beliefs. Beyond the Night Journey, it is viewed as a testament to the continuity of divine messages throughout history. The presence of the Foundation Stone, revered in Jewish tradition as the site of Abraham's near-sacrifice of Isaac (or Ishmael, according to Islamic interpretation), underscores the shared heritage of the Abrahamic faiths. However, in Islamic theology, the Dome of the Rock represents the culmination of these traditions with the advent of Islam and the prophethood of Muhammad. The Dome itself, with its golden facade and intricate calligraphy, serves as a visual declaration of Islam's final and complete message. The inscriptions from the Quran around the interior of the Dome explicitly proclaim the oneness of God and the prophethood of Muhammad. This assertion of Islamic supremacy further contributes to the site's sacred significance, marking it as the ultimate manifestation of divine will and the seal of the prophets.

extendedContent.title: Angels and the Divine Presence
extendedContent.content: Beyond its connection to Prophet Muhammad, the Dome of the Rock is also associated with a strong angelic presence. Islamic tradition holds that angels constantly surround the Foundation Stone, perpetually praising and glorifying God. These angels are believed to act as intermediaries between the earthly and heavenly realms, further enhancing the rock's status as a sacred nexus. Some accounts even suggest that the angels played a role in guiding the construction of the Dome itself, ensuring its alignment with divine principles. The constant presence of these celestial beings underscores the intense spiritual energy concentrated at this location. The belief in this angelic guardianship contributes to the sense of awe and reverence felt by pilgrims and visitors to the Dome of the Rock. The whispers and echoes within the structure are sometimes interpreted as the faint sounds of angelic prayers, adding to the mystical atmosphere surrounding the site.
